coredata.dc:title: Efficiently boosting the optical performances of laminated luminescent solar concentrators via combing blue-white light-emitting carbon dots and green/red emitting perovskite quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Chen
Wang, Mingzhi
Wang, Jianying
Xu, Bing
Wang, Jun
Zhang, Lei
Xin, Wei
Xue, Longjian
Li, Jinhua
Wang, Xianbao
coredata.dc:description: Full spectrum/white light-emissive luminescent materials play a key role in boosting the performances of luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs). In this work, a novel type of blue-white light-emitting carbon dots (BWCDs) have first been synthesized by one-step solvothermal strategy based on trisodium citrate and glutathione. The BWCDs solution show broad photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum from 400 to 700 nm and high PL quantum yields (PLQYs) of 13.82 %. A laminated BWCDs based polymer LSC, comprising glass/BWCDs based polymer film/glass, is then created via one-step photopolymerization technique. To simulating the sunlight spectrum, green/red emitting CsPbBr3/CsPbI3 quantum dots (G/RQDs) have further been synthesized and used to coat the bottom and top glass surfaces of laminated BWCDs LSCs, respectively, finally forming multi-emission laminated LSCs. The mutli-emission laminated LSCs (25 × 25 × 2.0 mm3) based on BWCDs and G/RQDs exhibit an external optical efficiency (η opt) of 3.76 %. Compared to the BWCDs based LSCs, ∼176 % enhancement in η opt has been realized. Besides, the multi-emission laminated LSCs exhibit a good transmittance of above 40 % over 520 nm of light wavelength.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Perovskite quantum dots
Luminescent solar concentrators
Multi-emission
External optical efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of BWCDs, green CsPbBr3 and red CsPbI3 QDs (G/RQD)
Characterization of the BWCDs and G/RQDs.
Optical properties and performances of single emission LSCs.
Optical properties and performances of multi-emission LSCs.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51673060, 11574075), Science and Technology Department of Hubei province (Grant No. 2020BIB020), and the Ministry of Science and Technology of China (Grant No.2016YFA0200200). Overseas Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation (D18025) also gave part of support. Xue thanks National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFB1105100) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (51503156).

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51673060 , 11574075 ), Science and Technology Department of Hubei province (Grant No. 2020BIB020 ), and the Ministry of Science and Technology of China (Grant No. 2016YFA0200200 ). Overseas Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation ( D18025 ) also gave part of support. Xue thanks National Key R&D Program of China ( 2018YFB1105100 ) and National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51503156 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of fabrication of multi-emission laminated LSCs based on BWCDs and G/RQDs.
(a,b,c) The HRTEM images of BWCDs, CsPbBr3 and CsPbI3 QDs, respectively. The inset is the corresponding lattice structure. (d) X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CsPbBr3 and CsPbI3 QDs.

(a − c) UV absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of BWCDs in ethanol, CsPbBr3 QDs and CsPbI3 QDs in toluene, respectively. (d) FTIR spectra of the BWCDs. (e) Full XPS spectra of the BWCDs. (f) PL decay spectra of BWCDs, G/RQDs under an excitation wavelength of 375 nm.

(a, c, e) Photographs of BWCDs based LSCs with different BWCD concentrations (from left to right: 0.4–13.9 wt%), GQDs based LSCs with different GQDs concentrations (from left to right: 4.4–52.4 wt%), RQDs based LSCs with different RQDs concentrations (from left to right: 5.3–42.9 wt%) under daylight, respectively. (b,d,f) Photographs of (a,c,e) under UV light (365 nm), respectively.

(a − c) UV absorption, transmittance spectra and PL spectra of BWCDs based LSCs with different BWCD concentrations. (d − f) UV absorption, transmittance spectra and PL spectra of GQDs based LSCs with different GQDs concentrations. (g − i) UV absorption, transmittance spectra and PL spectra of RQDs based LSCs with different RQDs concentrations.

(a − c) Relationship of the photocurrent density and photovoltage (J–V) of the BWCDs, GQDs and RQDs based LSCs (25 × 25 × 2 mm3), respectively. (d − f) The corresponding external optical efficiencies histogram of BWCDs, GQDs and RQDs based LSCs (25 × 25 × 2 mm3), respectively.

(a) Photographs of multi-emission LSCs (from left to right: BWCDs/RQDs, BWCDs&G/RQDs, BWCDs/GQDs) under daylight and UV light (365 nm), respectively. (b) Emission spectra of the BWCDs&G/RQDs based LSCs. (c) UV absorption spectra of dual/triple emission LSCs. (d) Transmittance spectra of dual/triple emission LSCs. (e) Relationship of the photocurrent density and photovoltage (J − V) of the dual/triple emission LSCs. (f) The corresponding external optical efficiencies of dual/triple emission LSCs.

head.ce:title: Efficiently boosting the optical performances of laminated luminescent solar concentrators via combing blue-white light-emitting carbon dots and green/red emitting perovskite quantum dots

School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Full spectrum/white light-emissive luminescent materials play a key role in boosting the performances of luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs). In this work, a novel type of blue-white light-emitting carbon dots (BWCDs) have first been synthesized by one-step solvothermal strategy based on trisodium citrate and glutathione. The BWCDs solution show broad photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum from 400 to 700 nm and high PL quantum yields (PLQYs) of 13.82 %. A laminated BWCDs based polymer LSC, comprising glass/BWCDs based polymer film/glass, is then created via one-step photopolymerization technique. To simulating the sunlight spectrum, green/red emitting CsPbBr3/CsPbI3 quantum dots (G/RQDs) have further been synthesized and used to coat the bottom and top glass surfaces of laminated BWCDs LSCs, respectively, finally forming multi-emission laminated LSCs. The mutli-emission laminated LSCs (25 × 25 × 2.0 mm3) based on BWCDs and G/RQDs exhibit an external optical efficiency (η opt) of 3.76 %. Compared to the BWCDs based LSCs, ∼176 % enhancement in η opt has been realized. Besides, the multi-emission laminated LSCs exhibit a good transmittance of above 40 % over 520 nm of light wavelength.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) have attracted much attention owing to their potential low-cost applications in building integrated photovoltaics (BIPVs) [1,2]. LSCs are the light-management devices, of which fluorescent materials are embedded/coated in/on a polymeric or glass waveguide. Fluorescent materials in LSCs absorb short-wavelength light and re-emit longer-wavelength light which further is guided to the LSCs edge where it is converted into electricity via PV cells [3,4]. To date, various fluorescent materials, including dyes [5], semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [6,7], perovskite QDs [8], carbon dots (CDs) [9,10] and others, had been applied in LSCs successfully. Their properties, especially, the photoluminescent (PL) emission range and PLQYs, greatly affect the performances of LSCs, such as the external optical efficiency (η opt) and power conversion efficiency (η PCE) [11]. So far, a majority of LSCs focus on usage of fluorescent materials with single PL emission [12–14]. To further improving the performances, development of the LSCs with multi PL emission (covering the entire visible light range) and high PLQYs are highly desired [15,16].

Comparing to LSCs based on single PL emission materials, LSCs with multi PL emission ability will further expand luminescence range and enhance their optical performances. Based on this idea, tandem LSCs based on two or three kinds of fluorescent materials with various luminescence emissions have widely been developed. First, two types of dyes (4-(dicyanomethylene)-2-t-butyl-6-(1,1,7,7-tetramethyljulolidyl-9-enyl)-4H-pyran) (DCJTB) and platinum tetraphenyltetraben-zoporphyrin (Pt(TPBP))) were used to fabricate tandem LSCs. The DCJTB showed PL emission from 550 to 750 nm and the PL emission range of Pt(TPBP) was located at 720–850 nm. Relying on this, ∼6.8 % of the η PCE was obtained [5]. Compared to dyes, semiconductor/perovskite QDs show more advantages such as high PLQYs and tunable luminescence across the visible and NIR wavelengths [17–19], enabling for highly efficient LSCs [20,21]. For example, two kinds of semiconductor QDs, including Mn:CdxZn1-xS/ZnS as top-layer and CuInSe2/ZnS as bottom-layer, were selected as fluorophores in tandem LSCs [6]. The broad PL emission of the top layer (∼500–750 nm) and bottom layer (∼600–1000 nm) resulted in ∼ 6.4 % of optical quantum efficiency and ∼ 3.1 % of η PCE of the as-fabricated LSCs. Recently, a novel luminescent material, carbon dots (CDs), have been created as promising fluorophores for applications in LSCs. Compared to dyes and QDs, CDs have their unique features, such as ease-synthesis, low-cost, eco-friendly, nonphotobleaching, high photostability, chemical inertness, tunable absorption and emission spectra crossing UV–visible-NIR wavelengths, and high PLQYs [22]. For obtaining highly efficient LSCs, CDs are first used to fabricate tandem LSCs via combining with other fluorophores, e.g., dyes, AIE molecules, QDs, etc. For instance, one tandem LSC were created by using CDs as the top LSC layer and organic dyes (e.g., Yellow 10GN and Red G) as the bottom layer. CDs at the top layer emitted yellow color (from 500 to 700 nm). Yellow 10GN and Red G at the bottom layer show maximum PL emission of 512 and 591 nm, respectively. Based on this, η opt of 16.32 % and η PCE of 3.2 % were achieved [21]. To solving aggregation-caused quenching of dyes, one solid-state luminescent materials, aggregation-induced emission (AIE) molecules, are used to obtain tandem LSCs by coupling with N-doped CDs together. For example, the N-CDs/PVP thin films were used to fabricated the top LSC layer, and the AIE molecules (TPFE-Rho)/PMMA nanocomposite slab was used for the bottom layer [23]. The maximum PL emission of N-doped CDs layer and AIE layer appeared at ∼ 430 and 573 nm, respectively. And the PLQYs of N-CDs and TPFE-Rho were up to 79.65 % and 34.88 %, respectively. Compared single layer LSCs (1.76 % of η PCE based on N-CDs and 2.3 % of η PCE based on AIE molecules), the tandem LSCs obtained the η PCE up to 4.06 % [23]. Furthermore, by using advantages of both QDs and CDs, combination of CDs and QDs (semiconductor/perovskite QDs) have been designed for tandem LSCs. For example, the CDs and CdSe/CdS QDs were used as the top and bottom emitting layer of LSCs with good photo-stability, respectively, resulting in ∼ 1.4 % of η opt which was 16 % enhancement in efficiency over the single layer LSCs based on CdSe/CdS QDs [24]. Besides, another tandem LSCs based on CDs and perovskite QDs (e.g., CsPb(BrxI1-x)3 and CsPb(ClxBr1-x)3 QDs) as fluorophores achieved a higher η opt (∼3%), which represents a 27 % enhancement in efficiency over single layer LSCs [25]. To further realize eco-friendly and low-cost LSCs, the tandem LSCs only based on three kinds of CDs have been created [9]. To maximizing the PL emission range, blue, green and red emitting CDs are chosen and used to fabricated the top, medium and bottom layers of the tandem LSCs, respectively. The as-fabricated tandem LSCs exhibited ∼ 2.3 % of η opt, which is more than 50 % enhancement compared to the single CD-based LSC. The tandem LSCs have make great progress, however, which still suffer some drawbacks such as complex structure, hard to integration, and low transmittance, etc., it is still a challenge for development of highly efficiency, simple structured LSCs with full color/white light-PL emission, high PLQYs, and good transmittance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BWCDs, green CsPbBr3 and red CsPbI3 QDs (G/RQD)
ce:section.ce:para: The BWCDs were prepared via a solvothermal synthesis method. 30 mg of reduced glutathione and 100 mg of trisodium citrate dihydrate were first dissolved in 10 mL ethanol. A uniform solution was obtained after stirring for 30 mins at room temperature. Then, the mixtures were transferred into a 50 mL stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave. Subsequently, a 180 °C ethanol-bath heating process was carried out for 4 h. The raw samples were first purified by centrifugation at 12,000 r/min for 10 min in order to remove lager precipitates. The above samples were then purified by dialysis (500 Da). Finally, the purified BWCDs could be obtained for further use after removing solvents and drying under vacuum. Preparation of CsPbBr3 and CsPbI3 quantum dots (G/RQDs) were synthesized by using a modified hot-injection method [26,27].

Highly transparent glass with different size (25 × 25 × 2 mm3, 25 × 25 × 1.5 mm3, 25 × 25 × 0.3 mm3) as substrate were first cleaned by ethanol, acetone, deionized water and ethanol under ultrasonic cleaning for 30 mins, respectively. A certain amount of BWCDs ethanol solution was added into hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA) monomer followed by evaporation of ethanol at 60 °C for 10 h to prepare BWCDs-HEMA suspension with different BWCDs concentrations (0.4 wt%, 2.3 wt%, 4.4 wt%, 9.2 wt% and 13.9 wt%). And then, a certain amount of 2-hydroxy-2-methyl-1-phenyl-1-propanone (HMPP) as photo-initiator and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) as crosslinker were added in the BWCDs-HEMA suspension. The above suspensions were then dropped and cast on glass substrates (25 × 25 × 1.5 mm3) and then covered with another glass sheet (25 × 25 × 0.3 mm3). After that, the laminated structure was polymerized under 365 nm UV light for 2 min to obtain the BWCDs based single emission LSCs.

For G/RQDs-based LSCs, poly(ethylene-co-vinyl acetate) (EVA) are chosen from four polymer (PS, PMMA, EVA, PVB) as polymer matrix to fabricate G/RQDs single emission LSCs since the EVA-based LSCs had higher performances (Figure S1). 1.5 g of EVA was first dissolved in 10 mL toluene until a uniform solution was formed after stirring at 70 ℃ for 30 mins. And then, various G/RQDs-EVA solutions with different G/RQDs concentrations (4.4 wt%, 8.7 wt%, 13.1 wt%, 26.2 wt% and 52.4 wt% of GQDs, 5.3 wt%, 10.7 wt%, 21.4 wt%, 32.2 wt% and 42.9 wt% of RQDs) were prepared by adding varied contents of G/RQDs into the EVA solutions. The G/RQDs-EVA mixtures were sonicated for 1 min and drop-casted on the glass substrates (25 × 25 × 2 mm3). The uniform and transparent G/RQDs-based single emission LSCs were achieved after the evaporation of toluene.

Dual-emission LSCs: The G/RQDs-EVA mixtures with a certain G/RQDs concentrations (13.1 wt% of GQDs, 10.7 wt% of RQDs) were sonicated for 1 min, and then drop-casted on the glass surface of the BWCDs based single emission LSCs (4.4 wt% of BWCDs concentrations). The Dual-emission LSCs (GQDs/BWCDs based LSCs, RQDs/BWCDs based LSCs) were fabricated after the evaporation of toluene.

Triple-emission LSCs: The as-prepared G/RQDs-EVA mixtures with a certain G/RQDs concentrations (13.1 wt% of GQDs, 10.7 wt% of RQDs) were drop-casted on the bottom and top surfaces of glass substrates of the BWCDs-based single emission LSCs, respectively. The triple-emission LSCs (GQDs/RQDs/BWCDs based LSCs) were obtained after the evaporation of toluene.

To characterizing the morphology and diameter of the BWCDs, G/RQDs, the high-resolution field-emission transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was conducted on Talos F200X (FEI, Czech). Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out on a NICOLET iS50 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The elemental analysis was obtained through X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) (PHOIBOS150, Germany). Photographs of all the LSCs under visible and UV light were monitored by a common camera. The UV–vis absorption spectra of BWCDs, G/RQDs were measured using UV–vis absorption spectrometer (UV-3600 Plus, Shimadzu, Japan). The PL excitation and emission spectra of BWCDs, G/RQDs were tested via the fluorescent spectrometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Japan). The steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and time resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) (under 420 nm excitation) was obtained by DW-PLE03 (Fluo Time 300). The current density–voltage (J-V) curves of the silicon solar cell were measured by a source meter (Keithley 2400, USA) under simulated AM 1.5 G (100 mW·cm−2 irradiance) provided by a solar simulator (Newport Oriel Sol 3A Class AAA, 64023A). Silicon solar cells (Voc : 0.54 V, Jsc : 30 mA/cm2, PCE:11 % (active area: 0.5 cm2), EQE:16 %) is purchased from Ningbo Angui Electronics Co., ltd. The PLQYs (η PL), edge emission efficiency (η edge) of all the LSCs were measured on fluorescence spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics QE65PRO-FL) with a calibrated integrating sphere. The external optical efficiency (η opt) can be calculated by using the following equation: η opt =
I
LSC
I
sc
∗ G
, where I LSC is the short circuit current from a cell attached to the LSC and I SC is the short circuit current measured for the same solar cell under direct illumination with the same light source. G is a ratio of the top surface area (Atop) relative to the edge surface area (Aedge) of the LSC. A majority of surface area of the silicon solar cell was covered by a black tape and the left surface area of the solar cell is strictly the same as one edge area of the LSCs for measurement of optical efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the BWCDs and G/RQDs.
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, BWCDs, green CsPbBr3 and red CsPbI3 QDs (G/RQDs) are used as fluorescent materials for constructing multi-emission laminated LSCs (Fig. 1). BWCDs were first synthesized by using glutathione and trisodium citrate dihydrate as the precursors via one-step solvothermal method. First, the morphologies and sizes of BWCDs and G/RQDs have been demonstrated by high resolution TEM (HRTEM) (Fig. 2 a-c). From the HRTEM image in Fig. 2a, the BWCDs have round spherical shapes. In the meantime, well-resolved lattice fringe of the BWCDs (0.21 nm) is clearly observed from the inset in Fig. 2a, which is related to the (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphene [28]. Second, the diameters of the BWCDs have been found to be ∼ 3.36 ± 0.84 nm after statistical analysis (Fig. S2a). Besides, a modified hot-injection is used to obtain green CsPbBr3 and red CsPbI3 QDs. As shown in Fig. 2b and 2c, the HRTEM images show a clear cubic shape and lattice fringe patterns, ascribable to the (110) plane of CsPbBr3 QDs and the (1–10) plane of CsPbI3 QDs [29,30]. Both CsPbBr3 and CsPbI3 QDs show the cubic phase from XRD pattern of Fig. 2d [27]. The particle sizes of CsPbBr3 and CsPbI3 QDs are ∼ 10.08 ± 1.25 and 8.11 ± 1.24 nm, respectively (Fig. S2b and S2c).

The optical properties of the BWCDs and G/RQDs have been demonstrated, as shown in Fig. 3 a-c. First, the BWCDs show one absorption peaks at ∼ 380 nm from the UV–vis absorption profile in Fig. 3a, which is attributed to the n-π* transitions of carboxyl or other aromatic structure in BWCDs [31]. While the absorption peaks of the GQDs and RQDs are at ∼ 300 and 320 nm, respectively. Their PL excitation spectra well match the absorption peaks. From the emission spectra, the emission peak of the BWCDs is located at 450 nm under 380 nm excitation, while the emission peak of the GQDs at 515 nm under 300 nm excitation and RQDs at 670 nm under 320 nm excitation are shown. Obviously, from the inset image of Fig. 3a-c and Figure S3, the BWCDs ethanol solution exhibit transparent and blue-white emission under illumination of daylight and 365 nm UV light, respectively. GQDs and RQDs solution appear green and red emissive color under irradiation of UV light. Furthermore, the PL dependence of the BWCDs and G/RQDs under various excitation wavelengths have been demonstrated in the PL spectra (Figure S4). Remarkably, BWCDs exhibit single PL emission, as the excitation wavelength increases (380 nm–520 nm), the PL emission peaks of BWCDs red-shift gradually, showing the typical excitation dependence phenomenon of CDs which probably come from the mutliple surface states [32]. The BWCDs solution exhibit strong blue-white emission when excited from 360 to 520 nm and the emission peaks show an obvious red shift from 430 nm to 590 nm. Thus, the BWCDs not only show broad excitation wavelength from UV to visible light, but have expanded PL emission range from blue to red, nearly covering the entire visible range. On the contrary, the GQDs and RQDs show the excitation independence behavior: all the PL emission peaks G/RQDs are located at 515 and 670 nm under various excitation wavelengths (from 260 to 480 nm), respectively. The results exhibit that the excitation independence behavior, probably resulted from the uniform size effect of G/RQDs. The large Stokes shift of fluorescent materials play a significant role in the performances of LSCs. Based on the above data, The Stokes shift of BWCDs (∼70 nm), GQDs (∼215 nm) and RQDs (∼350 nm) have been achieved.

To demonstrate the chemical structure of the BWCDs, The FTIR techniques have further been used to identify functional groups of BWCDs (Fig. 3d). The peak position at ∼ 3350 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibrations of –OH groups, and the peak at ∼ 1660 cm−1 is attributed to amide bond. Besides, the vibrations at ∼ 1390 cm−1 is originated from CN stretching and the peak at ∼ 1335 cm−1 from CO stretching peak. The results show that functional groups as hydroxyl and carbonyl groups are existed on the surface of BWCDs. Furthermore, XPS measurements are performed to identify the detail of chemical bonds and elements composition. As shown in Fig. 3e, there exist three kinds of elements, namely C, O and N, from three different kinds of peaks at ∼ 285 (C1s), ∼398 (N1s) and ∼ 530 (O1s) eV. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s further demonstrate the presence of CC (284.29 eV), CN (284.68 eV), CO (285.26 eV) and C-OH (287.91 eV) of BWCDs, respectively (Figure S5). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s further show the presence of pyridinic N (398 eV), amino N (399.3 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.1 eV) of BWCDs, respectively (Figure S5). Besides, to demonstrate the PLQYs of the BWCDs and G/RQDs, their PL decay spectra under excitation of 375 nm are shown in Fig. 2f. The PL decay curves of BWCDs and G/RQDs are fitted with triple-exponential decay kinetics and the lifetime data are presented in Table S1. The average lifetimes (τ ave) of the BWCDs, GQDs and RQDs reach up 4.51, 6.21, and 15.15 ns, respectively. Based on this, the BWCDs, GQDs and RQDs can obtain the high PLQYs of 13.82 %, 68.29 %, and 71.05 %, respectively (Table S1). Importantly, the BWCDs, GQDs and RQDs also exhibit good PL emission stability since their PL intensities can keep at least 90 % of initial PL intensities after one week (Figure S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and performances of single emission LSCs.
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 5, we can find that the increased concentrations of BWCDs and G/RQDs had stronger absorption ability (Fig. 5a, d, g), and more intense PL emission (Fig. 5c, f, i), which can harvest more sunlight and benefit the performance of LSCs. However, strong absorption ability will lower the transparence of the LSCs (Fig. 5b, e, h). For instance, the transparency gradually decrease from 90 % to 30 % for GQDs based LSCs when the GQDs concentrations are increased from 4.4 wt% to 52.4 wt% (Fig. 5e). Similarly, transparency for RQDs based LSCs drops from 65 % to 18 % once increasing RQDs concentrations from 5.3 wt% to 42.9 wt% (Fig. 5h). Thus, there is an optical concentration for construction of multi-emission LSCs with high performance and good transparence.

To demonstrate the performances, e.g., external optical efficiency (η opt), the typical J–V characteristic curves of the single-emission LSCs have been measured. First, the effect of BWCD concentrations on J–V characteristic curves have been investigated, as shown in Fig. 6 a and d. With the increase of BWCD concentrations from 0.4 to 13.9 wt%, η opt of 1.05 %, 1.23 %, 1.36 %, 1.21 %, and 1.09 % are obtained. The results show that there is an optimal case for BWCDs based LSCs at 4.4 wt% of CD concentration. By comparison, For GQDs based LSCs, the η opt of 1.01 %, 1.24 %, 1.66 %, 2.12 % and 3.08 % are obtained, respectively, when the GQDs concentrations are increased from 4.4 to 52.4 wt% (Fig. 5e). For RQDs based LSCs, the η opt of 1.71 %, 2.14 %, 2.19 %, 2.43 % and 2.55 % are obtained, respectively, when the RQD concentrations are increased from 5.3 to 42.9 wt% (Fig. 5f). The results exhibit that the enhanced performances are probably resulted from the increased absorption (Fig. 5d and g), and enhanced PL emission (Fig. 5f and 5i). The detailed photovoltaic parameters of the optimal BWCDs based and G/RQDs based LSCs are listed in Table S2, including values of V oc, J sc, FF, and η opt.

In addition, to further evaluate the optical efficiency, we have measured the internal quantum efficiency (η int) and external quantum efficiency (EQE, η ext) of the LSC by using an integrating sphere [35]. The detailed procedure can be found in the Supporting Information. As shown in Figure S7 and S8, the PLQY (η PL) of the BWCDs (4.4 wt%), GQDs (13.1 wt%) and RQDs (10.7 wt%) based LSC are calculated as 11.54 %, 35.91 % and 32.97 %, respectively. The edge-emission efficiency (η edge) of BWCDs, GQDs and RQDs based LSCs are 47.01 %, 51.91 % and 54.07 %, respectively. The internal quantum efficiency (η int) can be calculated according to the equation (1): [35].
(1)
η
int
=
η
PL
×
η
edge

the η int of the LSC is defined as the ratio between the number of photons emitted from the edges of the LSC and the number of absorbed incident photons. The η int of 5.42 %, 18.64 % and 17.83 % can be acquired for the BWCDs, and G/RQDs based LSCs, respectively. Furthermore, the external quantum efficiency (EQE, η ext) can be obtained according to the equation (2) [35], which is defined as the ratio of the number of photons emitted from the edges of the LSC to the total incident photons.
(2)
η
ext
=
η
int
×
η
abs

where η abs is the absorptance of the LSC at the incident-light wavelengths, which can be calculated using the equation: η abs=(1-R) ×(1-10−OD) (OD is the optical density at the specific wavelength and R is the reflectance (Figure S9)). Therefore, the η ext of BWCDs and G/RQDs based LSCs at the corresponding monochromatic light excitation (405 nm) are 1.70, 5.84 and 6.36 %, respectively. The detailed optical efficiencies of all single emission LSCs are summarized in Table S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and performances of multi-emission LSCs.
ce:section.ce:para: To further enhance the performances of single-emission LSCs, the multi-emission LSCs have been prepared by using BWCDs and G/RQDs as fluorescent materials, as shown in Fig. 7 a. Since high concentration of fluorescent material will greatly reduce the transmission of LSCs (Fig. 5b, e, h), herein, the 4.4 wt% of BWCDs, 13.1 wt% of GQDs and 10.7 wt% of RQDs are used for constructing dual-emission and triple-emission LSCs. As shown in Fig. 7a, the multi-emission LSCs also show good transparence and intense PL multi-emission. It can be seen clearly that the triple-emission LSCs emit red, white and green lights from the edges of the LSCs under UV illumination, well matching the spectrum of solar irradiation (Fig. 7b). By comparison with dual-emission LSCs, the triple-emission LSCs have stronger absorption and lower transmittance, as shown in Fig. 7c and 7d. Based on this, the dual-emission LSCs have 1.89 %, 2.54 % of η opt for BWCDs/GQDs, BWCDs/RQDs based LSCs, respectively. The triple-mission LSCs based on BWCDs&G/RQDs have 3.76 % of η opt (Fig. 7e, f). Compared to single-emission based LSCs, e.g., BWCDs based LSCs, 176 % of enhancement in η opt can be achieved for triple-emission LSCs. The detail photovoltaic parameters of the multi-emission based LSCs are listed in Table S4. More importantly, the multi-emission LSCs also exhibit good stability in performances since the multi-emission LSCs can keep 88.3 % of η opt after one week (Figure S10). It is noted that the multi-emission laminated LSCs also exhibit a good transmittance of above 40 % over 520 nm of light wavelength (Fig. 7d). To sum up, the combination of BWCDs and G/RQDs will lead to multi-emission LSCs with relatively high optical efficiency, however, there still exist some limitations. One is the main emission peak of BWCDs is located at 450 nm (blue range) which is not a pure white emission, the other is the GQDs have partly reabsorption owing to the overlapping of absorption and emission at ∼ 500 nm. These limitations will affect the performance enhancement of LSCs. Thus, to further solve the above issues and enhance the LSC efficiency, it is necessary to design and synthesize pure white emission CDs and perovskite QDs with large Stokes shift and high PLQYs in the future work [36].

In summary, one mutli-emission laminated LSC is created by using BWCDs, green, red perovskite QDs as fluorescent materials. The BWCDs with a wide PL emission range are synthesized via one-step solvothermal treatment of trisodium citrate dihydrate and glutathione. The multiple PL emission from BWCDs and G/RQDs can effectively simulate the spectrum of sunlight irradiation, thus, 3.76 % of η opt for the triple-emission LSCs (BWCDs&G/RQDs based LSCs) is obtained, showing 176 % of performances enhancement, compared to single-emission LSCs (e.g., BWCDs based LSCs). Importantly, the multi-emission LSCs also have good transparence and stability. Therefore, the multi-emission strategy shows great potential for development of advanced LSCs with high performances.

Chen Cai: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Mingzhi Wang: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Jianying Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Bing Xu: Visualization. Jun Wang: Formal analysis. Lei Zhang: Investigation. Wei Xin: Validation. Longjian Xue: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jinhua Li: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xianbao Wang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51673060, 11574075), Science and Technology Department of Hubei province (Grant No. 2020BIB020), and the Ministry of Science and Technology of China (Grant No.2016YFA0200200). OverseasExpertiseIntroductionCenterforDisciplineInnovation(D18025) also gave part of support. Xue thanks National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFB1105100) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (51503156).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2022.155313.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
